question: What is the main thread in Java, and how is it created when a Java application starts?
expectedPoints: The main thread is the initial thread that begins execution when a Java program starts.
It is created automatically by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM).
The main thread begins by invoking the main() method of the specified class.
It continues until the main() method returns or the program terminates.

question: Explain the lifecycle and default priority of the main thread. Why is it often referred to as a 'non-daemon' thread, and what is the implication of this?
expectedPoints: The main thread's lifecycle starts when the program begins and ends when the main() method exits.
Its default priority is Thread.NORM_PRIORITY (a value of 5).
It is a non-daemon (user) thread, meaning the JVM will not terminate until this thread finishes execution.
This is why if the main thread spawns other non-daemon threads, the JVM will wait for them all to complete before shutting down.

question: Describe how you can obtain a reference to the main thread object from within the main() method. Subsequently, explain the potential consequences if the main thread is explicitly set to a daemon thread and how this would affect the application's execution.
expectedPoints: A reference to the main Thread object can be obtained by calling Thread.currentThread().
The setDaemon() method can be used to change the daemon status of a thread, but it must be done before the thread is started.
Since the main thread is already running when main() begins, calling setDaemon(true) on it will throw an IllegalThreadStateException.

Hypothetically, if it were possible, setting the main thread as a daemon would cause the JVM to exit immediately after starting, as there would be no non-daemon threads running, likely terminating the application prematurely.
